segments.s434-g169: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from' introducing the cause. تیر گز is a tight compound in which گز qualifies تیر (a poetic/archaic pairing meaning a piercing/terse sense of ‘arrow’); there is no written ezafe here, but بند دستان زال functions as بندِ دستانِ زال — 'the fetter(s) of Zāl's hands' (بند = 'fetter/bond', دستان = 'hands', زال = proper name), showing the common epic omission of ezafe and the noun–possessor order with the possessor last.
segments.s434-g170: همی is the archaic/epic adverb signaling continuous or habitual action ('kept/continually'), مویه کرد(ند) is the past plural construction ('they made lament' → 'they bewailed'), and بسیار سال is a time-phrase placed after the verb meaning 'many years' (بسیار here = 'many', سال = 'year'). The implied subject is plural and carried by کردند.
segments.s434-g25: Subject + verb + goal phrase: تهمتن (proper epithet/name) is the subject; ببردش is an older narrative perfect/past form meaning 'he took him' (here expressed without an explicit auxiliary); به + ایوان = 'into/to the iwan' (به functioning as a directional preposition); خویش is the possessive base plus enclitic ش 'his', so ایوان خویش = 'his iwan'. Word order is typical Persian: Subject – Verb – Object/goal.
segments.s434-g26: همی is an archaic/aspectual particle often marking ongoing or vivid past action; پرورانید is the verb 'nurtured/raised' (past tense narrative here). چون + noun is a simile/comparison 'like/as'; جان خویش = 'his own life/soul' with خویش formed from خوی + enclitic ش. The clause modifies how Tahmatan treated the person (he raised him as if he were his very life).
segments.s434-g27: به + personal name = 'to Gushtasp'; آگاهی آمد is a standard construction meaning 'news/information came' (آگاهی as the subject, آمد as the verb); ز راه = 'from the road' (ز is the poetic 'from'). Word order: indirect object (به گشتاسپ) precedes subject and verb, which is common in epic Persian.
segments.s434-g28: نگون شد is a passive/ resultative phrase 'became down/felled' (نگون here = 'overthrown/downfallen'); سر is the direct object 'head'; نامبردار شاه = 'the renowned/aforementioned king' (نامبردار qualifying شاه). The verb شد is the past/resultative 'became/was', so the whole means the king's head had been struck down.
segments.s434-g29: همی is the narrative/aspectual particle (marks vivid past action); جامه را is the definite direct object ('the garment/robe' with را); چاک زد is a light-verb construction where چاک (to rend) plus زد (to strike/do) yields 'he tore/rent (it)'; بر is a preposition 'upon/at'; برش here is the noun following بر (in context 'upon him/his body'—the text uses a compressed epic phrasing). The clause describes the act of tearing the robe.
segments.s434-g30: به خاک = 'to/toward the earth' (به as directional); اندر آمد is a compound meaning 'came down/fell in(to)' (اندر = in/into, آمد = came); سر و افسرش = 'his head and his helmet' (افسر with enclitic ش = his helmet). The whole clause states that his head and helmet came down to the ground — a terse epic way to report a fall/decapitation and the helmet's dropping.
segments.s434-g31: خروشی برآمد = 'a cry/roar arose' (خروشی noun + برآمد verb 'issued forth'); ز ایوان = 'from the iwan' (source); به زار = an adverbial phrase 'in a lamenting/wretched manner' (به + زار, where به gives manner and زار is 'lament/wail'). This clause describes the sound issuing from the hall.
segments.s434-g32: جهان شد پر از ... is a common construction 'the world became full of ...' (جهان subject, شد = became, پر = full, از = 'of'); نام اسفندیار = 'the name/reputation of Esfandiyar' (نام here means fame/reputation). The clause means Esfandiyar's name (fame) filled the world.
segments.s434-g1: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning 'a certain' (not the numeral here). نغز is an adjective placed before the noun and means 'fine/beautiful' in poetic diction. تابوت is the object 'coffin' and کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/fashioned' (کرد = past of کردن); آهنین is a postpositive adjective modifying تابوت ('iron, made of iron'). Word order is essentially 'one (subject) + adjective + object + verb + adjective (postpositive)'.
segments.s434-g2: بگسترد is a past action 'he spread' (archaic/poetic past form). فرشی here names the thing spread ('a carpet' or rug). ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the material; دیبای is 'brocade/silk', and چین here denotes origin ('China' — i.e. Chinese brocade). The clause literally reads 'he spread a carpet of Chinese brocade.'
segments.s434-g3: بیندود is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'smeared/daubed' (here treated as the past action); because the provided sense list did not include that meaning, we mark the sense as unknown. یک روی آهن is a noun phrase 'one side (a single face) of the iron' with یک = 'one/a single' modifying روی ('surface/side') and آهن = 'iron'. به + قیر expresses the instrument/material 'with tar/pitch' (به here = 'with/by'). Overall: 'he smeared one side of the iron with pitch.'
segments.s434-g4: پراگند is a past verb 'he scattered' (or 'they scattered/poured'); بر is the preposition 'on/upon', followed by قیر 'pitch/tar' as the surface, then مشک 'musk' and و عبیر 'and ambergris' as the substances scattered. The clause therefore says 'he scattered musk and ambergris upon the pitch.'
segments.s434-g5: ز introduces the material 'from/out of' (ز دیبای زربفت = 'out of gold-brocaded silk'). کردش is the past verb کرد 'made' with the enclitic object ش ('him/it') attached, so کردش = 'they made for him' or 'they made him (a ...)'. کفن is the direct object 'shroud.' Structure: material phrase + verb(with clitic) + object.
segments.s434-g6: خروشان is an adverbial participle/adverb meaning 'roaring/tumultuously' (describing how they proceeded). برو is the verb 'they went/bore' (poetic past form of رفتن in context of motion). نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying انجمن 'assembly/meeting.' Word order places the manner adverb before the verb and the destination after it: 'with clamour they went to the renowned assembly.'
segments.s434-g7: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). بپوشید is a past narrative form 'they clothed/dressed (him)' (here reflexively or with object understood); روشن is the adjective 'bright' describing the garments. برش in context functions as 'on him' or part of the verb phrase meaning 'they clothed him', but the given sense list for برش does not match this use and is marked unknown. The clause means 'thereafter they dressed him in bright clothing.'
segments.s434-g8: ز here indicates material or ornamentation relation ('with/on account of'); پیروزه refers to the turquoise (the stone or turquoise‑inlaid decoration). بر is the preposition 'on', سر is 'head', نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put', and افسرش is افسر 'helmet' with the third‑person object clitic ش ('his helmet' or 'helmet upon him'). The clause says 'they set a turquoise (adornment) on his head as his helmet.'
segments.s434-g41: بزرگان ایران (literally “the great ones of Iran”) is a plural subject phrase (بزرگان modified by ایران). گرفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb meaning “took/experienced” (literally “they took”); خشم is the object (“anger”). The phrase گرفتند خشم is an idiomatic past construction meaning “became angry.”
segments.s434-g42: ز here means “because of / on account of.” The clause that follows uses an older word order in which the possessed phrase (گشتاسپ) appears before the verb: شستند چشم literally “they washed (their) eyes.” In this context شستند چشم is best understood idiomatically as “they averted/turned away their eyes” or “they refused to look,” and the plural verb agrees with the previously implied plural subject (the elders).
segments.s434-g43: به آواز is an adverbial phrase meaning “in a voice / aloud.” گفتند is past, 3rd-person plural “they said/shouted.” کای is a vocative “O,” and شوربخت is an address meaning “ill‑fated” or “wretched.” The whole segment reports an audible, addressed exclamation.
segments.s434-g44: چو is the comparative/simile “like / as.” اسفندیاری names Esfandiyār; تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun “you.” از بهر is a prepositional sequence meaning “for the sake of,” and تخت is “throne.” There is no finite verb here; the clause is a verbless comparison: “you (are) like Esfandiyār with respect to the throne,” an elliptical construction continued by the next lines.
segments.s434-g45: به زابل فرستی is a 2nd‑person verb phrase (فرستی “you send”) with indirect object/place زابل “to Zabul.” In classical Persian poetry a present/subjunctive form like فرستی can express a condition (“should you send / if you send”). به کشتن دهی uses the verbal noun phrase به کشتن “to killing” plus دهی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive of دادن) meaning “you cause/give,” so together “to send (someone) to Zabul to be killed” or “to have him killed.”
segments.s434-g46: تو is the explicit 2nd‑person subject. بر گاه here means “upon the seat/place” (گاه used poetically as place), تاج مهی is the epithet “the moon‑like crown” (مهی = moon‑like/beautiful), and برنهی is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person form indicating placement with a negative/resultative sense in context—together giving the consequence: “you will not set/place the moon‑like crown upon the throne.” The clause expresses the consequence of the preceding conditional.
segments.s434-g47: سرت را puts your head (سرت) with the object/contrast marker را. ز تاج کیان is a causal phrase “because of/from the crown of the Kians.” شرم باد is an optative formula: شرم “shame” + باد (optative) = “may shame be (upon you).” The whole is a curse/blessing construction: “May shame be on your head because of the Kayan crown.”
segments.s434-g48: به رفتن is a verbal noun construction “in (your) going / in departing.” پی means “after / in pursuit of,” and اخترت is اختر + the 2nd‑person suffix “your star.” نرم is an adverbial adjective “gently/softly.” باد again is the optative particle “may.” Together: “May your going in pursuit of your star be gentle.”
segments.s434-g49: برفتند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of رفتن meaning “they went/they departed.” یکسر is an adverb placed after the verb meaning “at once/altogether,” and ز ایوان او is a prepositional phrase (ز = poetic از “from”) specifying the place they departed from; او is the 3rd‑person possessive pronoun referring to the owner of the iwan.
segments.s434-g50: This clause has the predicate first: پر از خاک شد = “became full of dust,” where پر is an adjective “full (of),” followed by از + noun to mark the thing they are full of. شد is the past of شدن meaning “became.” کاخ و دیوان او (palace and court/office of him) is the subject placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s434-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” آگاه شد is a two‑word predicate meaning “became aware” (آگاه + شد). The noun phrase مادر و خواهران is the subject; in poetic usage the verb (شد) appears before the subject, and the singular شد is used here though the subject is plural — a tolerable poetic variation.
segments.s434-g52: ز ایوان is a prepositional phrase “from the iwan” using poetic ز (از). برفتند is the past 3rd plural “they departed.” با دختران is an accompanying‑agent phrase “with the maidens/young women”; با governs the noun دختران.
segments.s434-g53: برهنه is an adjective “naked/bare” modifying the coordinated body parts سر و پای (“head and feet”). پرگرد is a poetic verbal/participial idea meaning that dust was fluttering/whirling about them; it stands coordinated with خاک “dust,” so the phrase describes them as bare and surrounded/covered by flying dust.
segments.s434-g54: به تن = “on/over the body” (locative/dative expression). بر همه جامه literally “on all garments”; کردند is the past 3rd plural of کردن (“they did/made”), and چاک is the noun “a tear/gash.” Together: “they made (کردند) a gash/tearing (چاک) on all their garments,” i.e. they tore all their clothes.
segments.s434-g55: پشوتن is the proper name. همی is a classical adverb meaning “continually/still” (often used before verbs). رفت is the simple past “he went.” گریان is a participle/adverbial “weeping,” modifying the action. به راه = “on/along the road.” Word order places the adverbial phrases after the verb in this line.
segments.s434-g56: پس پشت is a compounded locative/relational phrase meaning “after/behind” or “in the rear of.” تابوت و اسپ سیاه enumerates what he follows: تابوت = coffin, و = and, اسپ سیاه = “the black steed” (adjective سیاه follows the noun in poetic order). The whole phrase says he went behind the coffin and the black horse.
segments.s434-g73: کزو is a relative/prepositional compound meaning "from whom" and introduces a source/agent; شاه را uses the object marker را to mark "the king" as the affected person; روز is an adverb of time "that day"; برگشته بود is the past perfect/pluperfect (برگشته + بود) meaning "had returned/been turned back" and ties back to کزو as the source (i.e. 'from whom the king had been turned back that day').
segments.s434-g74: به آورد is an ordinary past-tense clause with به + آورد ("brought/led to"), بر پشت او is a prepositional phrase "on his back" (او here = 'him/his'), and کشته بود is the past perfect of کشتن. In context کشته بود describes a completed violent outcome ("had been killed" or "had killed" depending on voice); here it functions as a perfect participial clause linked to the preceding clause, describing the state/result involving the one on the back.
segments.s434-g75: کزین پس is a fixed temporal phrase "from this (time) on / henceforth"; کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative pronoun "who/whom"; برد خواهی is a future construction (برد + خواهی) meaning "you will take/carry/lead" (here: 'take into'); به جنگ is an adverbial prepositional phrase "to battle." Together the segment means "henceforth, whom you take to war..."
segments.s434-g76: کرا again = "who/whom"; داد خواهی here is the verb stem داد (give) plus خواهی forming a future sense "you will give/hand over"; به introduces the beneficiary/recipient phrase; چنگ نهنگ literalizes as "the claw/hold of the whale" (چنگ = claw/hold, نهنگ = whale), so the whole clause means "whom you give you will hand into the whale's clutches."
segments.s434-g77: به یالش places the action at the target "at/against his mane" (به + noun), همی is the archaic continuous/progressive particle (often marking ongoing or habitual action), and اندرآویختند is a past plural verb "they grappled/struggled together". Word order puts the locative (به یالش) fronted for emphasis.
segments.s434-g78: همی again gives a continuous or emphatic nuance; خاک is the direct object "dust"; بر introduces the target "upon/over" and تارکش is a compound (تار + کش) meaning the "tar/ string-player" or the person associated with the tar (here treated as the target of the dust); ریختند is the past plural of ریختن "they poured/threw" — together: "they threw dust upon his tar-player/strings."
segments.s434-g79: به ابر اندر آمد is a locative-motion phrase "came into/rose into the clouds" (به + ابر + اندر آمد); خروش سپاه is a genitival phrase "the roar/clamor of the army" with خروش as head and سپاه as its possessor. The sentence order places the locative motion before the noun phrase for poetic effect: 'into the clouds came the army's roar.'
segments.s434-g80: پشوتن is a proper name (Peshotan); بیامد is the simple past form "came"; به ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase "to the king's iwan/porch" (به + ایوان + شاه). The verb agrees with the third-person singular proper name subject.
segments.s434-g57: زنان (subject) + prepositional phrase از پشوتن + past plural verb درآویختند. Here از introduces the target/point of attack (literally ‘at/on Peshotan’), and درآویختند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb meaning ‘they grappled/attacked’. The agent 'they' is the women (زنان) from the start of the segment.
segments.s434-g58: همی is a literary/progressive particle marking an ongoing or immediate action before the verb phrase. The core verb is فرو ریختند: فرو is an adverbial/prefix 'down(ward)' and ریختند is the past plural of 'to pour/fall', so the whole phrase means 'blood fell/was poured down'. ز + مژگان gives the source 'from the eyelashes'. The (implied) subject is the women from the previous clause.
segments.s434-g59: که here introduces the subordinate/quoted command ('that...') connecting to reported speech; این بند تابوت را is the object phrase 'this fastening/strap of the coffin' (بند = bond/strap), with را marking it as definite/object. برگشای is an optative/imperative form 'open' (poetic form of برگشودن/برگشستن) addressing the addressee.
segments.s434-g60: تن خسته is a noun phrase 'weary body'. یک‌بار is an adverb 'once/just once' modifying the command, ما را is the recipient/object 'to us' with را used as an object marker, and نمای is an optative/imperative poetic form of 'to show' (نمود/نمودن) meaning 'show (to us)'. Word order is flexible: the imperative (نمای) comes at the end, with objects and adverb placed before it.
segments.s434-g61: This is a simple past-clause: پشوتن (subject) + غمی شد, where غمی 'grief/sorrow' is the predicative noun and شد is the past-tense copula 'became'. میان زنان is a locative phrase 'among the women' specifying where/among whom he became a cause of sorrow.
segments.s434-g62: Two coordinated participial/descriptive elements describe the women: خروشان is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'wailing' or 'in tumult', and گوشت از دو بازو کنان is a gerundial phrase 'tearing flesh from both (their) arms' where از introduces source ('from the two arms') and کنان is a present-participial/gerundial form ('doing / tearing'), indicating the action in progress.
segments.s434-g63: به آهنگران گفت is 'he said to the smiths' (به + indirect object, گفت introducing reported speech). سوهان تیز follows as the content of that speech: 'a sharp file' (سوهان = file/rasp, تیز = sharp) — the object being requested in the reported command.
segments.s434-g64: بیارید is the plural imperative 'bring (you all bring)' addressing the smiths (literary form equivalent to بیاورید). کامد is an archaic/past-present verb meaning 'comes/has come' (here used as 'is coming' or 'has now come'), کنون is the adverb 'now', and رستخیز is the noun 'resurrection/uprising'. The two parts form the reported command and its urgent reason: 'Bring a sharp file; now the resurrection comes.' Note: the provided sense list did not include the imperative sense of بیارید, so that item is marked -1.
segments.s434-g129: ز ترکان is a prepositional phrase giving the source or target of the action ('from / against the Turks'). همی is the archaic auxiliary/adverb often used in epic Persian to mark the action (here giving a narrative/habitual flavor). کین is the object 'vengeance/hatred', او is the subject 'he', and بازخواست is used here as a verbal notion 'demanded / claimed' (i.e. he demanded vengeance). Word order follows older Persian: prepositional phrase + auxiliary + object + subject + verbal noun/verb.
segments.s434-g130: بدو is an indirect-object phrase 'to him'; شد is the main past verb 'became'; همی again is the narrative/ emphatic particle; پادشاهیت is the nominal predicate 'kingship' (kingship/the throne) and راست is a predicate adjective 'true/firm/established'. The construction literally reads 'to him became (then) kingship true' — i.e. 'the kingship became established for him.'
segments.s434-g131: به گفتار here introduces an instrumental/causal phrase 'by the speech/words (of)'. بدگوش modifies کردی: گفتارِ بدگوشِ کردی = 'the slanderous words of the Kurd'. کردی is the noun 'a Kurd' (not the 2nd‑person past verb). به بند begins the next phrase ('into fetters' / 'in bonds'). Note classical Persian allows noun + modifier order like this.
segments.s434-g132: بغل گران is a noun phrase 'a heavy/weighty embrace' (i.e. a constricting hold), here listed as one instrument of restraint. و links items; به + گرز و کمند is another instrumental phrase 'with (or by) mace and lasso'. The repetition of به before the next instruments is typical in listing means.
segments.s434-g133: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. او بسته آمد is a subordinate clause 'he came bound / he arrived in fetters' (بسته = bound/tied, آمد = came). نیا is the nominal subject that follows (here treated as a proper/name or title) and کشته شد is the passive 'was killed'. The clause order is classical: temporal clause → subject → predicate.
segments.s434-g134: سپه را is the definite-marked object/topic 'the army/host' (را giving emphasis/topic). همه روز is a time phrase 'the whole day / all day'. برگشته شد is a passive/intransitive phrasing 'was turned back / withdrew'. Together: 'the army was turned back all day.'
segments.s434-g135: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ارجاسپ is the proper name subject 'Arjasp' and آمد is the verb 'came'. ز خلخ به بلخ is a place/route phrase 'from X (خلخ) to Balkh (بلخ)'; classical verse often uses such paired place-phrases for sound and cadence.
segments.s434-g136: همه زندگانی is a nominal phrase 'all (one's) life / people's lives' (همه = whole/entire, زندگانی = life). شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became' and از introduces the cause 'from/because of'. رنج تلخ is the object/predicate 'bitter suffering' — literal 'suffering bitter', i.e. lives became full of bitter hardship.
segments.s434-g161: A simple main clause: the proper name (پشوتن) comes first (typical epic word order), then the adverb چنین 'thus', then the past verb گفت 'said'. با + مادرش is the prepositional phrase 'to/with his mother' (با here marks who he spoke to). The clitic ش attaches to مادر to mean 'his/her'.
segments.s434-g162: This is the direct speech clause introduced by the complementizer که 'that/that is, (or here introducing what he says)'.چندین 'many times/often' modifies the following phrase. به تنگی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with distress' (تنگی = distress/oppression). چه is an interrogative 'what/how' modifying کوبی; کوبی is an archaic infinitive/nominalized verb from کوبیدن here meaning 'to strike/beat', and در + ش (در = 'door' here) yields 'at his door'. The whole clause yields 'why do you so often, in distress, beat at his door?'.
segments.s434-g163: Relative/complement clause beginning with که 'that' (linking back to what was said). او 'he' is the subject, شاد 'happy' modifies the following predicate خفتست — an archaic fused form meaning 'is asleep' (خفت + است). و links روشن‌روان, a compound adjective 'bright‑soul' or 'radiant in spirit', describing his state.
segments.s434-g164: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. سیر here functions as a lexical element linked to آمد 'came' (either a proper name Sīr in epic usage or an archaic verbal/noun form meaning 'one who comes'); آمد is the past 'came'. از مرز و از مرزبان 'from the border and from the frontier‑guardian/guard' is a simple from‑phrase (از + noun). The clause gives the temporal circumstance: 'when Sīr/when he came from the border and the border‑keeper.'
segments.s434-g165: بپذرفت is a past tense verb 'accepted' (بـ- prefix + پذیرفت). مادر is the subject 'the mother'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source of the counsel: دین‌دار here functions as 'the pious one' (a religious person) and پند is 'advice/counsel'. The phrase = 'the mother accepted counsel from the pious man.'
segments.s434-g166: به داد خداوند is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by/according to the justice of God' (به + داد + خداوند). کرد is the past 'did/made'. او is the clitic pronoun 'him' (here referring to God), and پسند is a noun 'pleasing/approval' (object). Word order is somewhat inverted: 'she made it pleasing to God' / 'she did what pleased Him.'
segments.s434-g167: ازان پس 'from that time/thereafter' places the temporal reference. به سالی is an adverbial 'yearly' or 'each year'. به هر برزنی = 'at every barzan' (برزنی here = village square/threshing‑floor, a common gathering place in epic scenes). The sequence gives frequency and location: 'thereafter, each year, at every threshing‑floor...'.
segments.s434-g168: به ایران 'in Iran' marks the country where the action occurs. خروشی is a noun 'a loud noise/outcry' or 'commotion', modified by بد 'bad/terrible'. و links شیونی 'wailing/mourning'—together they describe public lamentation: 'a terrible outcry and wailing.'
segments.s434-g121: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. پردخته گشت is a passive/stative expression 'was/ became arranged/composed' (پردخته here = 'arranged, completed'), and از بزرگان سرای marks the agent/source ('by the great ones of the house/court'); the whole segment means 'when it was settled by the greats of the household.' Word order places the temporal particle چو at the front, then the predicate پردخته گشت, with the agent phrase introduced by از afterwards.
segments.s434-g122: برفتند is the simple past 'they went/they departed.' به introduces the destination 'to,' and here آفرید and همای are personal names (recipients of the action). The clause therefore means 'they went to Afarid and Homay.'
segments.s434-g123: به پیش پدر = 'before/in the presence of the father' (به + پیش = toward/in front of). بر بخستند is a past-tense verb complex from برخاستن 'to rise/lift' (here 'they lifted/turned up'), and روی ('face') is the object placed after the verb; literally 'they lifted their face toward their father.'
segments.s434-g124: ز درد برادر = 'from/because of sorrow for their brother' (ز = 'from/because of', درد = emotional sorrow). بکندند موی is a transitive past 'they pulled up/out (بکندند) hair (موی)' — a conventional Persian idiom for tearing out one's hair in grief.
segments.s434-g125: به گشتاسپ گفتند = 'they said to Gushtasp' (به marks the indirect object). کای نامدار is a vocative: کای = 'O' (addressing a king) and نامدار = 'renowned/illustrious' — together 'O renowned one (Gushtasp)'. The vocative follows the reported speech frame.
segments.s434-g126: نیندیشی is the negative imperative/optative 'do not think/consider' directed at the addressee (2nd person). از کار اسفندیار = 'about/regarding Esfandiyar's affair' (از = 'concerning/of', کار = 'affair/deed', اسفندیار = proper name). Together the clause is the reported admonition: 'Do not concern yourself with Esfandiyar's matter.'
segments.s434-g127: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. شد here is a past-tense/inchoative 'happened/was (done)' and with نخستین 'first' it asks where the first (one) was affected. به کین زریر literally 'by/with the vengeance of Zarihr' (به + کین = 'by/with vengeance'), so the clause asks 'Where was the first struck by Zarihr's vengeance?'
segments.s434-g128: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continually/used to'; گور بستد is a past verb phrase 'he closed/sealed the grave' (بستد = 'shut/close'). ز چنگال شیر = 'from/against the lion's claw' (ز = 'from/against', چنگال شیر = 'lion's talon'). The whole means 'he would seal the grave against the lion's claw.'
segments.s434-g9: سَر تَنگ تابوت کردند سخت is a compact poetic clause: سر + تنگ together describe the lid/top as 'tight' (سر = 'top, summit' used for the coffin's lid), تابوت names the object ('coffin'), کردند is the past plural verb 'they did/made', and سخت functions adverbially ('firmly, tightly'). Word order is inverted for poetic rhythm but parses as 'they made the coffin's lid tight/securely'.
segments.s434-g10: شد is the past-tense copula 'became'; آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'; بارور is an adjective 'fruitful/fruit-bearing'; خسروانی qualifies as 'royal, kingly'; درخت is the noun 'tree'. The clause means 'that royal tree became fruitful' with normal subject–predicate order (verb first for emphasis).
segments.s434-g11: چل is the numeral 'forty' modifying the following noun; اشتر here functions as the counted noun (in context: camels) though the lexical list gives a personal-name sense — in the line it is a noun meaning 'camels'. بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought', and رستم is the subject (Rostam) placed after the verb for metrical/poetic order. گزین is an adjective/participle 'chosen/selected' modifying the counted beasts.
segments.s434-g12: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from'; بالا = 'above, on high'; فروهشته is a past participle meaning 'let down, cast down, spread out' (here describing how the brocade was presented); دیبای = 'brocade/silk garment'; چین = 'fold(s)' (i.e. folded brocade). The prepositional phrase modifies how/what the camels carried: folded Chinese brocade brought from on high.
segments.s434-g13: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying اشتر (here 'camels'); بدی in this context functions as an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'were placed/put' (the lexical list's noun senses do not match this verbal use); زیر تابوت شاه is a prepositional phrase 'under/beneath the king's coffin' showing the placement. Word order (verb form near the noun) is compressed for verse.
segments.s434-g14: چپ و راست = 'left and right' (coordinate nouns); پیش و پس‌اندر = 'in front and behind' or 'before and after' (پس‌اندر is a compound adverb meaning 'after/behind'); سپاه = 'host/army'. The whole phrase specifies the camels' positions relative to the coffin and the army. Coordination is simple and idiomatic.
segments.s434-g15: همه 'all/everyone' is repeated for emphasis. خسته روی is an adjectival phrase 'weary-faced' (خسته 'tired' + روی 'face'); کنده موی is a participial construction 'having torn/pulled out hair' (کنده 'torn/uprooted' modifying موی 'hair'). The clause describes the mourners' physical state with two parallel adjective phrases.
segments.s434-g16: زبان here means 'speech/utterance' (not the anatomical tongue) and functions as the subject of an implied verb; شاه گوی is a compound meaning 'speaking of the king' (شاه + گوی, where گوی is related to 'speaking/saying'). روان is 'soul/spirit' and شاه‌جوی is a compound adjective/agent 'seeking the king' (شاه‌جوی literally 'king-seeker'). The two halves are parallel: their speech praises/mentions the king, and their souls long for him.
segments.s434-g17: بریده is a past‑participle/adjectival form 'cut off' placed before the possessive object; بش is an archaic/epic pronominal form referring to 'him' (object/possessive). The phrase بریده بش و دم اسپ سیاه literally strings two noun phrases together: 'the severed (thing) of him and the tail of the black steed' where دم + اسپ + صفت follow one another in the classical order.
segments.s434-g18: پشوتن is the proper name 'Peshotan' (subject). همی here functions as the classical continuous/imperfective marker (often translated 'was/kept on' or 'still'), combining with the verb برد to signal action. برد is the verb 'to take/bring' (3rd sg. form) and پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before the host/army' with پیش governing سپاه; the direct object (the head and tail) is omitted here by ellipsis and understood from the previous segment.
segments.s434-g19: برو is an imperative 'go'. بر + نهاده forms a participial circumstantial clause: بر (on/upon) + نهاده (past participle 'placed/put') describes an action done before/while going. نگونسار is an adjectival/poetic word 'woeful/mournful' qualifying زین (saddle); the phrase therefore means 'having put on the woeful saddle'.
segments.s434-g20: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. زین is the noun 'saddle' (object of ز). اندرآویخته is a perfect‑participle/adjectival verb 'hung/attached' used predicatively, and گرز کین is a genitival phrase 'the mace of vengeance' with کین postpositive specifying the mace's quality; the clause reads 'from the saddle hung the attached mace of vengeance.'
segments.s434-g21: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'the same' or 'that very'. نامور is an adjective 'renowned', and خود here is emphatic/reflexive 'himself' (i.e. 'the renowned one himself'). و is the conjunction 'and'. خفتان is a nominalized form from the verb 'to sleep' used poetically for 'sleeping‑gear/bed or sleep itself', and اوی is an old possessive/pronominal form 'his', so خفتان اوی = 'his sleeping (gear/bed)'.
segments.s434-g22: همان again 'the same/that very'. جوله here names an item associated with campaign or pack (classical term often 'saddle‑cloth/pack/foray' in context). و 'and' links items. مغفر is a noun 'covering/veil/cover' (here a protective covering such as a helmet or cap), and جنگجوی is a descriptive noun 'warrior' specifying whose covering it is ('the warrior's covering').
segments.s434-g23: سپه رفت is a simple past clause 'the army went/left' (سپَه subject + رفت past of رفتن). و links the next clause: بهمن به زابل بماند — بهمن is a proper name (Bahman), به زابل is a prepositional phrase 'in/to Zabul', and بماند (from ماندن) is a past/resultant form 'remained/was left'.
segments.s434-g24: به مژگان is a directional/locative phrase 'upon (his) eyelashes' (به governs مژگان). همی is the classical continuous/intensifying particle. خون دل is a compound 'blood of the heart' (a poetic expression for deep grief or inner bleeding), and برفشاند is a third‑person singular past/presentive of برفکندن 'to scatter/spatter/throw upon' — here 'he spattered/sprinkled (it) upon'.
segments.s434-g105: This is a past action formed with the past participle + auxiliary: کشت (past participle ‘killed’) + کردی (you did) = ‘you killed/slain’. یکی (literally ‘one / a certain person’) is the indefinite object placed before the verb; اندر جهان is a locative phrase ‘in the world’. The pronoun تو appears after the verb for emphasis (‘you’).
segments.s434-g106: که introduces a result/clause (‘so that/with the result that’). The clause says that ‘no one’ (کس here used negatively) ندرود — literally ‘greeted/praised’ — neither آشکار (‘openly’) nor نهان (‘secretly’). Note that in older poetic usage ن‑ can mark negation on verbs, so ندرود conveys failure to greet/praise; آشکار and نهان are adverbs framing public/private praise.
segments.s434-g107: بزرگی (‘greatness’) is the patient/subject; به گفتار تو (‘by your speech/words’) expresses the means or agent (به + noun = ‘by/through’). کشته is the past participle ‘killed’ and شد functions as the auxiliary forming the passive (‘was killed’).
segments.s434-g108: که introduces the result clause ‘that…’. روز بزرگان here means ‘the day/era of the great ones’ (روز = era/time); همه گشته شد is a passive/result phrase: گشته (past participle ‘gone/turned’) + شد (auxiliary) = ‘was altogether made/was ended’ — i.e. the era of the great ones was entirely finished.
segments.s434-g109: آموختی here is the verb ‘you taught’ (not ‘you learned’); شاه را marks the definite object with را (‘the king’). راه کژ is a noun + adjective order ‘a crooked way/path’. The clause literally: ‘You taught the king a crooked way.’
segments.s434-g110: This is an exclamatory/descriptive phrase about the ‘pīr’ (old man/elder). ایا functions as an emphatic/archaising particle (roughly ‘indeed/oh’). A string of adjectives follows — بی‌راه (‘off the path/wrong’), کوتاه (‘short’/brief or curt), و کژ (‘and crooked/wrong’) — describing the elder’s faults; adjectives follow the noun in this classical order.
segments.s434-g111: تو گفتی که = ‘you said that…’ (تو = you; گفتی = ‘you said’ in classical usage; که introduces the reported content). هوش یل is a genitive-like phrase ‘the intellect/skill (هوش) of the hero (یل)’ and اسفندیار is the name identifying that hero.
segments.s434-g112: بود is the past copula ‘was’; بر کف literally ‘on the palm’ (کف = palm/of the hand) is an idiom meaning ‘in someone’s hand/control’. رستم نامدار (‘Rostam the famed’) is the locative possessor: ‘was in the palm of famed Rostam.’ The adjective نامدار follows the name to qualify it.
segments.s434-g153: This is a nominal clause whose head is جهاندار (sovereign). پیش از تو is a prepositional/time phrase 'before you' modifying when/relative position. بسیار بود is the predicate: بسیار here functions as 'many' (a predicative quantifier) and بود is the past copula 'was/were'. Word order is subject + adverbial + predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s434-g154: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'), linking back to جهاندار. بر تخت شاهی is a prepositional expression 'on the royal throne' (تخت modified by شاهی). سزاوار بود is the predicate of the relative clause: سزاوار = 'fit/worthy' and بود is the past copula. The whole clause describes those sovereigns as fit for kingship.
segments.s434-g155: به + infinitive (کشتن) forms a goal/recipient phrase 'to killing / to be killed'. ندادند is the past negative of دادن 'they did not give'. فرزند را is the definite object (را marks it). Literal order here is 'to killing did not give the child' = 'they did not hand the child over to be killed'; the verb precedes the object (poetic flexibility) but را still marks the object.
segments.s434-g156: نه begins a coordinated negation 'nor'. از introduces a source/reason phrase 'from/out of'. دوده خویش is a compact, somewhat poetic expression meaning 'their own (lineage/house/kin)' (خوی + enclitic ش = 'his/their own'); پیوند را 'and (their) bond' is coordinated with را marking the definite object. The verb from the previous clause is understood to apply here as well ('nor did they [do it] from their own kinship and ties').
segments.s434-g157: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said' (reporting speech). پس is a connective 'then'. با + proper name (با پشوتن) indicates the addressee 'to/with Peshotan'. که introduces the reported imperative that follows. خیز is an imperative 'rise!' (a direct command).
segments.s434-g158: برین is the poetic spelling of بر این ('on/against this' or 'upon this'); آتش is the noun 'fire'; تیزبر is an epithet attached to آتش meaning 'sharp/keen' or 'fierce' (poetic compound). آب ریز is an imperative-like phrase meaning 'pour/sprinkle water' (آب = water; ریز here functions as a command 'pour/sprinkle' or as a verbalizing form). Together the phrase is an imperative instruction: 'pour water on this fierce fire.'
segments.s434-g159: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. The proper name پشوتن follows the verb (verb–subject order, common in narrative). ز ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's iwan/hall' indicating origin/place from which he came.
segments.s434-g160: زنان را is the definite object 'the women' (را marks definiteness). بیاورد is a third-person form 'he brought' or 'to bring' (here it functions with the motion verb to express purpose/sequence: 'he came to bring / and brought'). زان جایگاه is a locative 'from that place' (زان = 'from that'; جایگاه = 'place'). The sequence gives 'he came from the king's hall to bring the women from that place.'
segments.s434-g113: بگفت این ("he said this") places the verb بگفت before the following clause; و is the simple coordinating 'and'. گویا here functions adjectivally/adverbially meaning 'eloquent' or 'expressive', and زبان برگشاد is a semi‑idiomatic phrase 'opened his tongue' = 'began to speak'. The clause therefore reads: 'He said this, and, speaking eloquently, opened his tongue.'
segments.s434-g114: همه پند و اندرز او کرد یاد is a compact Persian construction where یاد کردن/کردن means 'to cause to be remembered' or 'to remind/recall'. همه پند و اندرز = 'all advice and counsel' is the object; او is the indirect object ('him'/'his'), کرد is 'made/caused', and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' used with کرد to form 'reminded' or 'recalled'.
segments.s434-g115: هم at the start means 'also/likewise'. اندرز بهمن (literally 'the advice of Bahman') is the content, followed by به رستم 'to Rostam' and the verb بگفت 'said' (here 'gave/speaked'). Persian often orders object/phrase before the verb, so this reads 'Bahman also offered counsel to Rostam.'
segments.s434-g116: برآورد is a past action meaning 'brought forth/revealed' (here 'disclosed'); رازی is 'a secret'; the relative/complement clause که بود از نهفت 'which was hidden' uses که 'that' and بود past 'was', while از نهفت (lit. 'from hiding') describes the secret as previously concealed.
segments.s434-g117: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. اندرز او 'his counsel' is the object placed before the subject (شهریار) — Persian permits object–subject–verb order or variations, here the subject follows to emphasize 'the king'.
segments.s434-g118: پشیمان شد is a two‑word predicate: پشیمان 'regretful' + شد 'became' (past of شدن), so 'became regretful'. از کار اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase 'because of/about the deed of Esfandiyar' giving the cause/object of his regret.
segments.s434-g119: پشوتن بگفت = 'Peshotan said/told'. آنچ (آن چِ) is a relative pronoun 'that which/what', and بودش is بود + enclitic ش 'it was' (past), so آنچ بودش نهان = 'what it had been that was hidden' i.e. 'what had been hidden'. The phrase is a direct object of بگفت.
segments.s434-g120: به آواز is an adverbial 'in/with a voice' (i.e. aloud); به here marks the manner ('by/with'). با شهر یار جهان attaches to indicate direction/recipient 'to/with the king of the world' — together: 'in a voice to the sovereign of the world' (i.e. he spoke aloud to the king).
segments.s434-g137: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; ما را is the direct object 'us' placed before a relative clause for emphasis; که introduces the relative clause 'که پوشیده داریم روی' = 'which we keep covered'; پوشیده داریم is a present/state construction ('we keep/hold ... covered') and روی is the head noun 'face' placed after the clause in this poetic word order.
segments.s434-g138: برهنه is an adverb/adjective 'bare/ exposed' describing how they are brought; بیاورد is third‑person singular (classical narrative form) 'brings' (here relative to the implied subject); ز ایوان به کوی is a movement phrase with poetic prepositions z = 'from' and به = 'into/towards', so 'from the iwan into the alley'.
segments.s434-g139: چو introduces a time clause 'when'; نوش‌آذر زردهشتی is the subject 'Nowsh‑Āzar the Zoroastrian' (name + epithet); بکشت is an archaic verbal form where the preverb ب‑ attaches to کشت (kill) and functions as a past action 'he killed' (3rd person singular).
segments.s434-g140: گرفت is a past‑tense verb 'seized/took'; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'then/at that time' (آن functioning as 'then' with زمان); پادشاهی is the object 'the kingship'; به مشت is an instrumental phrase literally 'in/with (his) fist' (به used idiomatically 'by/with').
segments.s434-g141: تو دانی is present 'you know'; که introduces the content clause; فرزند مردی is a genitive construction 'the son of a man' (the noun followed by its qualifier), and چه کرد is an embedded interrogative/exclamatory 'what did (he) do' with کرد as past 'did'.
segments.s434-g142: برآورد here is a narrative past meaning 'he brought/caused to come out' or 'he drove forth' (classical usage); ازیشان = از ایشان 'from them/out of them'; the three nouns دم و دود و گرد are a coordinated list 'breath and smoke and dust' (و is the conjunction 'and'). Grammatically, برآورد governs ازیشان and then lists the things produced/expelled.
segments.s434-g143: ز رویین دژ آورَد is a prepositional phrase + verb 'from the iron citadel he brought'; ما را is the direct object 'us' with the object marker را; برت is an archaic clitic/adverb meaning 'upon you / to you' (classical ordering places it after the object), so the whole says 'he brought us from the iron fortress to you'.
segments.s434-g144: نگهبان کشور is a noun phrase 'guardian/guard of the country' (noun + genitive); بد is an adjective 'bad/evil' qualifying the guardian; و is the conjunction 'and'; افسرت (classical form of افسر/افسرَت) here functions as 'officer/commander' in apposition. The line lists the addressee's role and qualities.
segments.s434-g145: This is a compact prepositional phrase + verbal clause in which 'از ایدر' = 'from here' (poetic 'ایدر' = here), 'به زابل' = 'to Zabul', and 'فرستادیش' is the past tense verb 'he sent' with the enclitic object pronoun '-یش' = 'him'. Word order is prepositional phrases first, verb last, typical of the epic style.
segments.s434-g146: 'بسی' here functions as an adverb meaning 'many/much' modifying the following nouns; 'پند و اندرزها' is a coordinated noun phrase 'advice and counsels' (note the plural suffix -ها on اندرز). 'دادیش' is the past verb 'gave' with the enclitic object '-یش' = 'him' (so literally 'he gave him').
segments.s434-g147: 'که تا' introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). 'از پی تاج' is an idiomatic prepositional sequence meaning 'in pursuit of the crown' ('از' + 'پی' = from/following/in pursuit of). 'بیجان شود' places the adjective 'بیجان' ('lifeless') before the verb 'شود' ('become'); 'شود' here has a potential/optative force ('might become').
segments.s434-g148: The subject 'جهانی' ('the world') is followed by two coordinated predicate phrases: 'برو زار شود' (literally 'go/become wretched') and 'پیچان شود' ('become twisted/confused'); 'برو' is used idiomatically with 'زار' to form 'become utterly wretched'. Again the verb 'شود' ends each predicate.
segments.s434-g149: The triple 'نه ... نه ... نه ...' is a negative coordination: 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. 'کشتش' is the past verb 'killed' with the enclitic object '-ش' = 'him' attached; the three names are listed as potential agents who did not kill him.
segments.s434-g150: Subject 'تو' = 'you' followed by the verb 'کشتی' (here a past 2nd‑person 'you killed') and two objects: the indirect 'مر' = 'to/for me' and the direct 'او را' ('him' marked by the object marker 'را'). 'چو' introduces a comparison 'as/like', then the verb repeats and 'منال' is the compared object. Note that the printed lemmas for 'کشتی' and 'منال' are nominal in the wordlist, but here 'کشتی' functions as the verb 'you killed' and 'منال' is a name/accusative in the simile.
segments.s434-g151: The phrase 'ترا شرم بادا' is an optative wish 'may shame be on you' where 'ترا' (here split in the wordlist as 'ت' + 'را') is the old accusative/oblique 'to you', 'شرم' = 'shame', and 'بادا' is the optative particle 'may it be'. 'ز ریش سپید' is an ablative/explanatory phrase 'on/from your white beard' (i.e., addressing the old man with the white beard).
segments.s434-g152: The relative/reason particle 'که' introduces the reason: 'that' / 'because'. 'فرزند' = 'child/son' is the object, and 'کشتی' here is the past verb 'you killed' (the supplied lemma senses are nominal, so the verb sense isn't listed there). 'ز بهر' = 'for the sake/sake of' and 'امید' = 'hope/ambition'. The clause accuses the addressee of killing his son out of hope/ambition.
segments.s434-g97: بگفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (third person). این is the demonstrative 'this' (referring to what was just spoken). و simply links two actions ('and'); رخ سوی جاماسپ کرد is a verb phrase where رخ 'face' is the thing acted on, سوی 'toward' marks direction, جاماسپ is the addressee, and کرد is the light/past auxiliary 'made/did' used with رخ to mean 'turned (his) face toward'. The whole segment = 'he said this and turned his face toward Jamasp.'
segments.s434-g98: که‌ای here introduces the quoted address and functions like a vocative ('O ...'); شوم functions as an evaluative/adjectival word 'ill‑omened' rather than a first‑person subjunctive here. بدکیش and بدزاد are epithets 'ill‑fated' and 'ill‑born' respectively, joined by و 'and'; مرد is the general noun 'man/person'. Together the phrase is a direct vocative: 'O ill‑omened, ill‑fated, ill‑born man!'.
segments.s434-g99: ز گیتی is a poetic locative/partitive 'in/from the world'; ندانی is second‑person present/past negative 'you do not know'; سخن 'speech' is the object; جز 'except' introduces the only thing known; دروغ is the exception 'falsehood/lie'. The clause means 'you know nothing in the world except lies.'
segments.s434-g100: به ... گرفتی: به + noun here marks direction/turning 'into' or 'by means of' (into crookedness), and گرفتی is the second‑person past 'you seized/you took'. ز هرکس marks the source 'from every person' and فروغ 'radiance/glory' is the thing taken. The segment states that, by crookedness, the addressee has taken away everyone's radiance.
segments.s434-g101: میان marks the setting 'among/between'; کیان is the plural 'kings' or 'royal house'; دشمنی is the noun 'enmity'; افگنی is an archaic/poetic second‑person present form meaning 'you cause/you cast/you sow' (causative). Together: 'You sow enmity among the kings.'
segments.s434-g102: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'continually/always'. The string that follows uses archaic prefixing: ب + دان (ب as the verbal prefix + دان 'to consider/turn') yields the sense 'make/turn X into Y'; syntactically it expresses 'you make this into that'. آن and the following (ب + دین) are the correlative 'that'/'this' elements; برزنی ('the plain' or 'public square') gives the location — i.e. you keep turning things around publicly. In short: 'you keep turning this into that and that into this in the public square.'
segments.s434-g103: ندانی is 'you do not know/you do not understand'; همی adds the sense of habitual/continuous action 'always'; جز 'except' introduces the sole thing known; بد modifies آموختن so that آموختن here means 'to learn' — together 'you always know nothing except learning bad things' (i.e. you only learn evil).
segments.s434-g104: گسستن ز نیکی is 'to break away/separate from goodness' (گسستن taking the sense 'separate/break off'), ز marks source 'from', نیکی is 'goodness'. بدی توختن pairs bad/evil (بدی) with the archaic verb توختن meaning 'to take up/embrace' or 'to make/turn to' — so the clause contrasts leaving goodness and adopting evil: 'breaking from goodness and embracing evil.'
segments.s434-g89: شکسته شد is a passive/resultative construction: شکسته (past-participle 'broken') + شد (the auxiliary) => 'was broken'. این نامور is a demonstrative + epithet 'this famed (one)'. پشت تو follows as a genitive phrase 'your پشت' where پشت here means 'support/protection' (an idiomatic use: someone's پشت = their backing). Word order places the object/phrase before the verb-auxiliary, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s434-g90: کزین پس = 'from this time onward' (کز + این + پس). بود ... باد here is an archaic/poetic optative formula: the copula بود combined with باد gives an efffect like 'may it be' or 'let it be'. در مشت تو is a locative phrase 'in your fist/grasp'. Taken together: 'from now on may (something) be in your grasp' (باد functions as the optative particle here).
segments.s434-g91: پسر را is a fronted definite object (پسر + object marker را). به خون دادی is an idiom: دادن + به خون = 'to give to blood' → to cause bloodshed / to have (him) slain. از بهر تخت = 'for the sake of the throne' (از بهر = because of/for). Note the verb دادی is 2nd-person-past 'you gave/caused'.
segments.s434-g92: که introduces a purposive/clausal complement here ('so that / in order that'). مه before تخت is an adjectival/epic epithet meaning 'mighty' or 'great' modifying تخت. بیناد is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'may (he) found/establish' (the verb appears in a jussive/optative sense; the copula is omitted). چشمت مه بخت is an elliptical nominal clause: چشمت 'your eye' and مه بخت 'great-fortune' (an adjectival compound), i.e. 'your eye [= your fortune] (be) great' with the verb omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s434-g93: جهانی functions as 'the world' / 'this realm'. پر از دشمن = 'full of enemies' (پر + از + noun). و پر بدان is a short, elliptical addition: پر means 'full', and بدَان is an older fused locution meaning 'with that / because of that' (built from به + آن); thus 'and full because of that'. The phrase lists two parallel descriptions of the world.
segments.s434-g94: نماند is the negative past of ماندن 'to remain' → 'will not remain / did not remain' (used here with general/future sense). بتو = 'for you' / 'with you'. تاج = 'crown'. تا جاودان 'until eternity' / 'forever' (تا = 'until', جاودان = 'eternal'). Word order places the negated verb before the indirect object and the noun, typical in poetry.
segments.s434-g95: بدین here is an archaic contraction of به این 'by/with this' (presented in the text split as ب + دین). گیتیت (گیتی) = 'the world / realm' and در نکوهش بودن = 'to be in reproach / to be censured'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole clause means 'by this your world was in reproach.'
segments.s434-g96: به‌روز شمارت is a fixed poetic locution: به‌روز 'on the day' + شمارت 'of your counting/ reckoning' → 'on your reckoning-day'. پژوهش بود = 'there was inquiry/investigation' (پژوهش = investigation; بود = 'was'). The clause states that at the reckoning-day an inquiry/count was made.
segments.s434-g65: This is a single past action: the object phrase 'سر تنگ تابوت را' (the narrow/top of the coffin) comes before the verb, and 'را' marks it as a definite direct object. 'باز کرد' is the simple past of the compound verb باز کردن (to open), with 'باز' here as the verbal element and 'کرد' as the past-tense auxiliary.
segments.s434-g66: 'به نوی' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in a new way / anew'; it modifies the following noun phrase. 'یکی مویه' is a noun phrase 'a single/a certain lament' (یکی used as an indefinite numeral/particle), and 'آغاز کرد' is the past-tense verb 'began' (آغاز کردن), with 'کرد' the past auxiliary.
segments.s434-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'مادرش' is 'his mother' (مادر + enclitic ش). 'با خواهران' is a prepositional phrase 'with the sisters'. 'روی شاه' = 'the face of the king' (classical word order places the noun 'روی' before the possessor).
segments.s434-g68: This continues the description seen by them: 'پر از مشک' is an adjectival phrase 'full of musk' (پر + از + noun). 'دیدند' is the past plural 'they saw'. The verbless noun phrase 'ریش سیاه' ('a black beard') is coordinated with the preceding description as another thing they saw.
segments.s434-g69: 'برفتند' is the simple past plural 'they went/rose'. 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'all at once / straightaway' that modifies the verb; 'ز بالین شاه' is a classical prepositional phrase 'from the king's bedside' (ز = from, بالین = bedside/at the side of).
segments.s434-g70: 'خروشان' is a participial/adverbial form meaning 'roaring, wailing' that describes the manner of their movement. 'به نزدیک اسپ سیاه' is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the black steed' (به نزدیک = toward/near, followed by the noun phrase 'اسپ سیاه').
segments.s434-g71: 'بسودند' is a classical past plural verb meaning they moved/turned toward or attended to something (an older usage; here it expresses their action of going up to the horse). 'پر مهر' is an adjectival phrase 'full of affection' modifying the following objects 'یال و برش' = 'the mane and its flank/side' (یال = mane; برش here = portion/side). The phrase as a whole describes an affectionate action toward the horse's mane and flank.
segments.s434-g72: This is a coordinated clause: 'کتایون' (Katayun) is the subject; 'همی' is the iterative/progressive particle marking a repeated or ongoing action; 'ریخت' is the past form of ریختن here meaning 'scattered/threw' (an action of casting); 'خاک از برش' = 'dust from its side' (she shakes/scatters dust off the flank), a mourning gesture. Grammatically, همی + past verb gives an iterative or continuous past sense.
segments.s434-g81: خروشید is a 3rd-person past intransitive (‘a roar rose/it roared’). و coordinates the next action. دیدش is past tense ‘saw’ with the enclitic object -ش (‘him/it’). In this compressed poetic line the two following nouns, نبردش and نماز, are juxtaposed as what was seen (literally “his battle, [and] prayer”), a terse apposition that here conveys that the person came either in fight or in prayer — the juxtaposition is a poetic ambiguity.
segments.s434-g82: بیامد is simple past ‘came’. The prepositional phrase به نزدیک marks motion toward proximity (‘to/near’). تختش is تخت + possessive clitic -ش ‘his throne’. فراز follows as a postpositive adverbial/epithet meaning ‘on high / aloft’; the order (نزدیک تختش فراز) reflects poetic word order putting the height word after the noun.
segments.s434-g83: به آواز گفت is a light prepositional phrase + verb meaning ‘said aloud / cried out’ (به + noun indicating manner + گفت ‘said’). ای is the vocative particle ‘O’. سر + سرکشان here is a genitive/epithet: سر ‘head/chief’ followed by سرکشان ‘the rebellious (ones)/rebels’ — together ≈ ‘O head of the rebels’. (سرکشان is a poetic plural/attribute.)
segments.s434-g84: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / because of’. برگشتن is the noun/masdar ‘returning’ or ‘reversal’. بختت is بخت ‘fortune/fate’ + possessive -ت ‘your’. آمد is past ‘has come’ and نشان is the noun ‘sign/token’; together ز برگشتن بختت آمد نشان = ‘a sign has come from/that your fortune has turned’ (i.e. a sign of the reversal of your luck).
segments.s434-g85: ازین here is from/through this (از + این) and functions adverbially ‘by this’. با تن خویش = با + تن ‘with the body/person’ + خویش (here written split as خوی + ش) meaning ‘your own person’. بد کرده‌ای is perfect construction: بد ‘badly’ + کرده‌ای (perfect of کردن with 2nd‑person singular ending) → ‘you have done (it) badly / harmed yourself’. The clitic -ای on کرده‌ای marks ‘you’.
segments.s434-g86: دم از is an idiomatic phrase where دم (‘breath/moment/utterance’) + از (‘about, concerning’) introduces the theme of an utterance or cry. شهر ایران is ‘the city of Iran’. برآورده‌ای is perfective of برآوردن ‘to raise/bring forth’ with 2nd‑person ending, here ‘you have raised/uttered’ (i.e. you have raised a cry about Iran).
segments.s434-g87: ز تو دور شد is a verbless‑looking clause headed by شد ‘became/was made’: ز تو ‘from you’, دور شد ‘went away/was distant’ — so ‘has departed from you’. فره is the technical royal/divine glory (‘farr’). و links فره and بخردی; here شد governs both nouns, so it means ‘the farr and wisdom have gone/are gone’. بخردی is the noun ‘wisdom/prudence’ (poetic form).
segments.s434-g88: بیابی is the subjunctive/optative form of یافتن ‘to find’ (here: ‘may you find’ / ‘you will find’). تو is the emphatic pronoun ‘you’. بادافره ایزدی is a compound epithet: بادافره ‘wind‑borne / fleeting’ modifying ایزدی ‘divine/god‑given’ — together a poetic phrase meaning a transitory or wind‑borne divine fortune; word order places the epithet after the pronoun for emphasis.
segments.s434-g33: This is a compact prepositional frame giving where the news traveled: به ایران (to/into Iran) marks the destination; ز هر سو (from every quarter) uses poetic ز for 'from' and هر سو is modified by the relative clause که رفت ('which/where he went'). The head noun آگهی ('news/announcement') completes the clause and is understood as the thing that traveled to Iran.
segments.s434-g34: بینداخت is a past-tense transitive verb ('threw off/removed'); in poetry the verb precedes the subject here, so بینداخت هرکس = 'everyone threw off' or 'each person removed'. کلاه مهی ('cap moon‑like') is the object phrase, with the adjective مهی following the noun in poetic order to mean 'the moon‑like (i.e. splendid) cap.'
segments.s434-g35: همی گفت is an iterative/habitual past construction meaning 'kept saying' (همی adding continuous or iterative aspect to the past verb گفت). گشتاسپ is the speaker put after the verb (poetic inversion). کای is a vocative 'O,' and پاک دین is an epithet 'pure of the faith' (پاک modifies دین).
segments.s434-g36: که introduces the content clause (what he says). چون here is comparative 'as/like' introducing the phrase 'چون تو نبیند' — 'time and earth have not seen the like of you.' نبیند is a literary present/subjunctive form used here with negative sense 'have not seen'; زمان و زمین are the compound subject placed after the verb (inverted poetic order).
segments.s434-g37: پس از روزگار منوچهر باز is a temporal phrase: پس از ('after') + روزگار منوچهر ('the era/days of Manuchehr') with باز ('again') providing the sense 'ever again / again.' The whole phrase sets the time frame for the following negative statement.
segments.s434-g38: نیامد is the simple past negative 'did not come' (here meaning 'none came/appeared'). چو تو نیز means 'like you also / such as you' (چو = 'like/as'), with نیز adding 'also' or 'indeed' to emphasize similarity/uniqueness; گردنفراز is an epithet 'proud, haughty' applied to the addressed person and placed after the clause in poetic style.
segments.s434-g39: This is a pair of verb phrases joined by و: بیالود تیغ literally says he kept the sword 'un‑stained/unsullied' (بیالود used as an adjectival/predicative notion) and بپالود کیش is a hortative/past form 'he purified the faith/creed.' The b‑ prefix on پالود is the old Persian/prepositive form used in epic (بپالود = 'he purified').
segments.s434-g40: مهان را (مهان = 'guests/great ones') with the object marker را marks the direct object/topic; همی داشت is an iterative past ('kept, maintained'); بر جای خویش means 'in/at their own place,' where خویش is the possessive 'his/their own' (here reflexive/possessive). The whole segment says he kept the nobles/guests in their proper place.
